[00:00:02] INTERVIEWER: So, whenever you're ready — maybe start by telling us what's changed for you this year.

[00:00:08] SARAH CHEN: Okay, so I guess I would start with — sorry, can I start that again? I lost my thread.

[00:00:15] SARAH CHEN: The moment I knew something had really changed was when a boy who had never finished a book in his life stood up in front of the class and read his own story out loud. That was the moment. Everything we'd been working toward was in that one minute.

[00:00:34] SARAH CHEN: I'm Sarah Chen, and I teach Year 5 at Westbrook Primary. It's a small school, about three hundred kids, and a lot of our students come in well below the expected reading level. I've been teaching here for eleven years, so I've seen a lot of programs come and go.

[00:01:02] INTERVIEWER: And what was the situation like before you made the change?

[00:01:08] SARAH CHEN: Before this year, I had a group of eight or nine students who had essentially given up on reading. They'd mastered the art of looking busy. They'd hold the book, they'd turn the pages, but nothing was going in, and worse, they'd decided that reading just wasn't for them. Once a ten-year-old decides that, it's very hard to undo.

[00:01:45] SARAH CHEN: So what we did was flip the whole thing around. Instead of starting with the books, we started with their own stories. Every student recorded themselves talking about something they cared about — football, their nan, a video game, whatever it was. We turned those recordings into printed books with their names on the cover. Their words, their book. And then the reading practice happened on material they already owned in their heads.

[00:02:30] SARAH CHEN: There's one student, I'll call him M. He came to me reading at a Year 1 level, and he was angry about it, really angry. His first book was about his dog. It was four sentences long. But he read those four sentences to anyone who would listen. By the end of term two he'd written and read eleven books, and his mum told me he reads to his little sister at bedtime now.

[00:03:10] INTERVIEWER: That's lovely. And did you see that reflected in the data as well?

[00:03:16] SARAH CHEN: We did, and honestly the data surprised even me. Across the target group, reading levels rose an average of fourteen months in a single school year. Two students moved from the intervention list to the extension group. And attendance on library days, which used to be our worst day of the week for that group, is now our best.

[00:03:50] SARAH CHEN: Sorry, I should mention — the library thing is funny actually, because the librarian used to hide the beanbags on Thursdays, that's a whole other story, you'd have to ask her about the beanbag situation, it became a bit of a saga.

[00:04:20] SARAH CHEN: What I've learned is that the barrier was never ability. The barrier was that these kids had no reason to believe the page had anything to do with them. The moment their own lives showed up in print, the whole relationship changed. You can't teach a child to love reading. You can only show them that reading already belongs to them.

[00:04:55] SARAH CHEN: People ask me if I'd go back to the old way, and I just laugh. I've got a class full of authors now. Last week M asked me if he could start a book club. A book club. That boy wouldn't touch a book twelve months ago, and now he's recruiting members. That's not a reading program. That's a changed life.

[00:05:30] INTERVIEWER: That's wonderful. Thank you, Sarah — I think we've got everything we need.
